```suggestion
- If there is no leader lock in the cluster, the running primary acquires the lock. If there is more than one primary node, then the first primary to acquire the lock wins. If there are no primary altogether, Patroni does not try to promote any replicas. There is an exception in this rule: if there is no leader lock because the old primary has demoted itself due to the manual promotion, then only the candidate node mentioned in the promotion request may take the leader lock. When the new leader lock is granted (i.e. after promoting a replica manually), Patroni makes sure the replicas that were streaming from the previous leader will switch to the new one.
```

Normally we prefer to have a single test file for each container/component combination. We think it strikes a good balance between writing isolated tests but also ensuring a particular container/component combination works as expected when integrated together. The fact that they are split into a container/component is (mostly) an implementation detail that we hide from the tests.

In this case we would have a feature_flag_page_test instead of both feature_flag_page_component_test and feature_flag_page_container_test.

I mention it here specifically because for these tests where you introduce TestableComponent, you could instead test by constructing a FeatureFlagPageContainer and specifying appropriate values for your ngrx selectors and then checking that the UI renders as expected.

I think the use of `None` as a placeholder for default is a common, well-working, abuse of Python kwargs where it should rather be:

```python
_undefined = object()

def x(value=_undefined):
  if value is _undefined:
     value = some_default
```

`None` isn't really used as a default placeholder but has a convenient way of saying "if you didn't pass the kwarg then we'll detect it by checking for `None` being the value". Since you never expect users to pass `None` as a valid value because your type is not `Optional`, that works most of the time — even it's a little abusive.

To circle back to this, that's why I don't think it's a real problem. Not passing a kwarg is valid and gives you the correct result. Passing `None` explicitly is invalid (break typing), like passing `1234` or `"foobar"`.

Digging further:  since PRs #436, #441, and #443,  `Client._patch_resource`, `Client._put_resource`, and `Client._post_resource` are the only three places where the `Connection.api_request` method is called with a `data` argument.  Therefore their callers are the only ones which could potentially use the `DEFAULT_RETRY_IF_ETAG_IN_JSON` policy.

There are a few cases where `Client._post_resource` is called with `data` passed as `None`:  otherwise, `data` is always a `dict` instance.  So, I think we could simplify `is_etag_in_json` to just:

```python
    return data is not None and "etag" in data
```

The various upload mechanisms might still end up passing in `data` as bytes at a lower level, but retrying based on an `ETag` is never sensible for them:  they all create new versions of the blob, and `ETag` is not a writable property.

Good question! I've thought about this, and my personal opinion is:

Before, the api-versions of dependent resources on are fixed and independent, so it might be better to Keep the api-versions which support edge zone independent.
Otherwise, there will be several problems:
1. If the api-version of dependent resources is upgraded, the VM related tests need to be recorded, which increases the coupling.
2. The api-version used by dependent resources may tend to be stable and fixed. I think this is the reason why the previous api-version used by dependent resources is the lowest version. [code link](https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/blob/dev/src/azure-cli/azure/cli/command_modules/vm/_template_builder.py#L72)
3. If other profiles also need to support edge zone in the future, if the api-versions of their dependent resources have not been upgraded in that profile, but we need to give higher priority to support VM and VMSS, then we can only bump version for all the dependent resources together. This workload is very heavy and it is not conducive to split.

In addition, similar situations have been dealt with in this way before: [code link](https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/blob/dev/src/azure-cli/azure/cli/command_modules/vm/_template_builder.py#L147-L155)

As for why not add the check for api-version, it is because `--edge-zone` has the api-version control `min_api='2020-12-01'` [code link](https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/blob/dev/src/azure-cli/azure/cli/command_modules/vm/_params.py#L103)

```suggestion
- repo: local

        description: Format files with clang-format.
```

Personally, I think the sequencing approach reduces the complexity (but perhaps I'm biased from writing so many behavior trees over the years).

There's some complication that arises from using an explicit rule, which is that some of the ops available at runtime will be dynamic (based on what frameworks you have installed: NCCL, DDL, etc.), while others will be available given some parameter state, and still others (as you mention) will be available on a per-tensor basis (which is available to the Enabled function via the Response parameter).

Using an explicit rule, the operation manager becomes very complicated with preprocessor code, needing to couple itself to what frameworks are installed (because certain ops will not exist at compile time).  Using the sequential approach, all of that is isolated to a single function in `operations.cc`, which I think makes the separation between "what ops are available at compile time" and "what ops are available at runtime" more clear.

after reading through this one more time I think this function should be decomposed as follows.  My reasoning is that boolean flags like this are an indication that there is some inner functionality that isn't properly separated, thus requiring a feature flag to be able to re-use that functionality.

Here's where I think the lines should be.

1. Outermost API that the user interacts with is responsible for loading the JSON and validating it.  All remaining functions assume inputs are valid.
2. `hash_domain(structured_data)`:
    - simple wrapper for `keccak(encode_data("EIP712Domain", structured_data["types"], structured_data["domain"]))`
3. `hash_message(structured_data)`:
    - simple wrapper for `keccak(encode_data(structured_data["primaryType"], structured_data["types"], structured_data["message"]))`


Probably good to have some high level nice error messages if any of the `"types"` / `"domain"` / `"message"` / `"primaryType"` keys are missing.

I find that whenever you find yourself writing a loop like this where the first iteration needs to be treated specially, it's often the case that restructuring your code to avoid the duplication can lead to a much cleaner result. In this case, there's a couple potential cleanups you can make which will come together into a nice overall result:
* passing a param set to an empty string will act as if you hadn't sent that param at all. So on your first iteration you could have `'pageToken': ''`, which means you don't have to worry about selective in passing in / modifying your params only on the second and future iterations
* you do some juggling to make sure `content['items']` contains the full list -- in this case, it's easier to just pull the data out of `content` and into a collector after each API request (which could also be something you could do for all iterations rather than just the ones after the first!)

Pulling that all together, you could end up with something like this:
```python
params = {'prefix': prefix, 'pageToken': ''}
items = []
while True:
    content = await self.storage.list_objects(self.name,
                                              params=params,
                                              session=session)
    items.extend([x['name'] for x in content.get('items', list())])

    params['pageToken'] = content.get('nextPageToken')
    if not params['pageToken']:
        break

return items
```

Python runs a global process called Semaphore Tracker that is used across processes spawned with the multiprocessing API.  If a process spawned by multiprocessing is hard killed while holding semaphores, then they will not be properly cleaned up and will be effectively leaked.  Note that this is only a problem at test time, as in practice the process that is hard-killed will not be the child of yet another process.

Because we aren't using BLAS to compute the minimum over the kernel (it's significantly faster to not use BLAS and let the compiler optimize this kernel). This means that `min_value > nan == False` and we have to handle `nan` explicitly. This used to be significantly slower than not handling them because the compiler didn't vectorize. It's more similar now, but there is still a penalty for dealing with `nan`.  We can also break the loop in case of a `nan`, which brings the timings closer together (especially if there are a lot of `nan`s), but that optimization slows down the general case of being nan-free.

 [Sidenote: On windows compilation uses default flags, I'd assume the same holds for linux/unix, which is AVX. We could get a bit more performance on benchmarks overall if we'd allow compilation with AVX2/AVX512 or similar instruction sets if present.]

Sorry, I forgot that the default `archs` would mean that Python 3.6/3.7 build nothing on arm64. 

The question of the default is a tricky one. My view when this decision was made is that we're designing defaults for a world where arm64 and x86_64 runners coexist. The best situation is to build+test x86_64 natively and arm64 natively, rather than using emulation - if we can help it. 

I'm still assuming that we're going to get arm64 runners on the major CI providers at some point. At that point, I think that the recommended configuration will have both a x86_64 runner and a arm64 runner. So I think we should design defaults that 'just work' for that.

That's where the current defaults come from. Since CirrusCI only has M1 runners, I can see that we might include an option in our example config: `CIBW_ARCHS: x86_64, arm64, universal2`.

The question of whether to default building both `universal2` and `arm64` on the arm runner is still open, in my mind. I'm still mostly of the opinion that building both is good, because universal2 packages are preferred in some contexts (e.g. distributable GUI apps), and that most packages are small and the extra build time isn't much of a hit. Of course, people building big libraries would be wise to change this to just build arm64.

Good point. I was just playing around with Python-3, because always wanted to start using `Pathlib` and Python-3 only features more. I think `os` is an old library, so maybe it's slightly more Pythonic to use `Pathlib`, but I'm not sure. I'm open with doing whatever for consistency, but here are a few posts in favor of `Pathlib`:
* [**Why you should be using pathlib**](https://treyhunner.com/2018/12/why-you-should-be-using-pathlib/)
* [**No really, pathlib is great**](https://treyhunner.com/2019/01/no-really-pathlib-is-great/)
* [**Python 3's pathlib Module: Taming the File System**](https://realpython.com/python-pathlib/)
* [**Is pathlib a viable replacement for os.path?**](https://python-forum.io/Thread-Is-pathlib-a-viable-replacement-for-os-path)
* [**Easier Python paths with pathlib**](https://www.linuxjournal.com/content/easier-python-paths-pathlib)
* [**Why use the Pathlib Module?**](https://stackabuse.com/introduction-to-the-python-pathlib-module/#whyusethepathlibmodule)
* etc. I just [Googled `Pathlib` vs. `os`](https://www.google.com/search?q=os.path.join+vs+pathlib)


There is one solid benefit to using `Path` objects over os, which is that it has the `open` method built in, so the old pattern:

    with open(os.path.join('path', 'to', 'file')) as f:
        # do stuff with f

becomes

    with (Path('path') / 'to' / 'file').open() as f
        # do stuff with f

which seems a little clearer perhaps? You can see this in action right here:
https://github.com/pvlib/pvlib-python/blob/d635c09123319c68182898001712377089199744/pvlib/test/test_pvgis.py#L61-L64

Note that if `__run_graph__/__op_graph__` refers to the op graph at run
level, and `foo/__op_graph__` refers to the op graph with user-provided
name `foo`, then there’s still a potential collision if the user chooses
to provide the name `__run_graph__`.

> the run-level graphs (which could be op or conceptual or both)

Run-level graphs are only ever op graphs. By “run-level graphs” we mean
“those graphs attached to a run by virtue of being stored on the
`graph_def` field of an `Event` proto, with no corresponding tag”. The
`graph_def` field is always interpreted as a `GraphDef`, never a Keras
model or anything like that. New-style op graphs, conceptual graphs,
profile graphs, etc. are all written as summaries that _do_ have tag
names.

The writer shuold of course make sure not to write conflicting data for
the same tag name, but run graphs don’t natively _have_ a tag name, so
we have to synthesize one—thence the potential for collision.

Anyway, yes, we can of coruse decide on the naming for the other parts
of the hierarchy when it’s actually needed.

A type should be marked `EngineAwareReturnType.cacheable=False` if it should be re-computed in each `Session`: see https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/cc4e1800416dadbf631f06799f840ec3242dadc0/src/python/pants/engine/engine_aware.py#L66-L72

The strategy here is to use that facility to cause _consumers_ of git types to be forced to re-run: so if `GitWorktree` is marked `cacheable=False`, any `@rule` which consumes it should re-run in each session, iff `GitWorktree` has an `__eq__` implementation which will cause it to not be equal (I'll comment elsewhere with that fact).

While this type could in theory also be marked, I think that the expectation is that only the `GitWorktree` should be directly consumed, and that this type is an implementation detail.

----

But yea: this is definitely a fairly fragile way to expose these types: it is easy to get wrong. But it feels worthwhile to expose them internally first with this API and see how that goes.

> I think we should update the current implementation which is designed according to work with a buggy kernel.

I am not sure if it's fair to say that the current implementation is designed to work with a buggy kernel.

The current implementation uses a set of APIs that are provided by many POSIX platforms, including Linux, macOS, BSD. 
It does not use [MRT6_ADD_MFC_PROXY](
https://github.com/torvalds/linux/blob/3d5e28bff7ad55aea081c1af516cc1c94a5eca7d/include/uapi/linux/mroute6.h#L32) which is only provided by latest Linux to add a (\*, G) MFC entry, which is not well documented and not proven to work (I had a version using MRT6_ADD_MFC_PROXY but it didn't work as intended).

That being said, I am not against a future enhancement that utilizes MRT6_ADD_MFC_PROXY for Linux kernel versions that has working  (\*, G) MFC. But we should keep this implementation for portability.

I was working on the same issue on torchaudio, and although I understand that this solves the issue, I wonder if there is a better way.
The problem here is `environment: image_name: "..."` overwrites the `environment` defined in `binary_common`. It seems to me that the ideal implementation is that `image_name` should be appended to the set of `environment` key-value pairs defined in `binary_common`, so that the downstream jobs do not have to re-define them. But with quick searching on YAML syntax, I cannot find such a way.
On the other hand, `binary_common` was defined for binary build process (probably by @seemethere ) and I just re-used it in unit test jobs without knowing much about it in the initial implementation of CCI job configurations (in torchaudio, which was later ported to torchvision). 
Environment values like `UPLOAD_CHANNEL` and `PYTORCH_VERSION` have become an essential part of unit test scripts in the previous release work, https://github.com/pytorch/vision/pull/2846, so ideally we want environment variables to be defined in one place and we want them to be automagically propagated.
The use of Docker adds extra layer to this as it seems inevitable to manually pass environment values to the container which runs the test.

I'd prefer immutable types. In Python `set` is mutable, while [`frozenset`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html?#frozenset) is immutable.

The only things holding me back from using `frozenset` are that:

- `frozenset` is a bit more exotic than `set`, so fewer users will be familiar with it
- `set` has a less-verbose syntax and representation than `frozenset`: `{1,2,3}` vs `frozenset({1, 2, 3})`, so we'll be seeing a lot of `frozenset({...})` typed out in documentation, examples, and tests

However, for the last point, `frozenset([1,2,3]) == {1,2,3}`, so we could use the lighter `set` syntax for tests, at least.

Also, if we do this for **unordered** output, we should probably make the **ordered** output immutable. We'd just need to make sure we're outputting `tuple` instead of `list` everywhere else.

At first glance, I'm more in favor of @ericl's initial composition suggestion rather than subclassing, for a few reason beyond the generic "prefer composition over inheritance" principle:
1. We only want to switch between implementations for a few transformations, not how the Arrow block is represented or how its state is mutated, so isolating that into a "transform" abstraction makes more sense to me.
2. We may want to dynamically switch between transformation implementations based on global config, or at-runtime overrides/dataset-specific automatic configuration, which I think is cleaner to do if dispatching in the method call rather than dispatching at block accessor construction time.

The `reading` would never be a `Msg` object. Going step by step: the `Msg('read', ...)` yielded by `bluesky.plan_stubs.read(...)` on the right side of `=` goes to the RE, is dispatched by the command lookup table to `RunEngine._read(msg)`, which calls `reading = obj.read()`, and then calls `plan.send(reading)` to send that `reading` back to the plan, where it lands on the left side of the `=`.

If the plan is consumed by something that does not `.send`, then the left side of the `=` just gets `None`.

This only comes up in the context of adaptive plans. (Many plans do not capture the result of `yield from`.) The `None` can be interpreted as, “Whatever is consuming this plan apparently isn’t actually getting readings from hardware. It might be a simulator or summarizer. We can’t actually do adaptive logic without readings, so here is `None` as a placeholder.” This behavior has been in place since early days.

in .net there is no explicit validation against any schema on deserialization for json or protobuf. you would most want to do this against the reader schema (the schema supplied to the deserializer). i should ideally be doing that. validation against the writer schema seems overly paranoid. 

although i do no explicit validation against the schema, in .net the schema is defined implicitly through attributes on the class, and the json library will do some validation due to that. but we support two types of attributes - those from the JSON library and those from the JSON schema library, and the latter aren't validated against (easy to add without breaking API change).

what i don't know the most about is the need for a schema registry instance. you might want a schema_registry client instance for two reasons. validate against writer schema. maybe some paranoid people want to turn this on. auto-register reader schema, if unknown to SR. maybe some people want that. I don't know. I don't think i'd use it. in .net, confusion about this is not an issue as we can add it later without breaking the API. in python that seems less possible. Overall i'm comfortable with the approach as it stands (document the sr param is ignored). Not ideal, but probably not a big deal.

> When does this situation come up? It doesn't seem okay to ignore a duplicate actor creation request.

Actually, this logic is similar to the logic on the worker side.

Consider that if some of actors are created already in cluster
and went well. Suddenly, the GCS Server is crashed. The following steps will be:

1. GCS Server will restart and load all actor_table_data from storage and try reconstruct all of actors.
2. GCS Server will try to lease worker from the node the actor previously tied to.
3. GCS Server will try to create the actor on the leased worker.

If the actor is already created on the worker in current raylet, then the raylet should fast reply the received LeaseWorker request come from GCS Server just and lease nothing because that the actor is already created on a worker in this node before the GCS Server restart.

That was the conclusion I came to. The logigcal place to add support for following redirects in Fido, would be in [prepare_request_for_twisted](https://github.com/Yelp/bravado/blob/master/bravado/fido_client.py). The retuened value from that in turn goes into [fido.fetch](https://github.com/Yelp/fido/blob/master/fido/fido.py) which attempts to look like the interface for requests, but isn't fully implemented (as demonstrated by the lack of allow_redirects parameter. For the record, I have no clue how the call to fido.fetch works in fido_client.py as it seems to be missing an import.

In summary, the effort required to make fido work with redirects is much more significant that getting requests to disable redirects by default. Given the bug I initially found where following redirects causes bravado to complain about unexpected HTTP statuses, I'd say that support for following redirects at all should probably be removed in the future or it should handle redirects at a higher level, e.g. response returns redirect, so now you should expect the response(s) from the target of the redirect. As not all redirects have a single target according to the spec, that gets even more complicated to handle at a library level, so I'd argue that's an implementation decision for the user of bravado to deal with.

```python
import time

a = dict(enumerate(range(100)))
n = 10_000_000

    try:
        a[100]
    except KeyError:
        continue

for _ in range(n):

        continue
t1 = time.time()
print(f'{t1 - t0:.3f}')
#> 0.646
```

[NoReturn](https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.NoReturn) is meant to signal that a function never returns normally. [Mypy's docs have a nice explainer](https://mypy.readthedocs.io/en/stable/more_types.html#the-noreturn-type)

> Mypy provides support for functions that never return. For example, a function that unconditionally raises an exception:
> ```python
> from typing import NoReturn
> 
> def stop() -> NoReturn:
>     raise Exception('no way')
> ```


It was useful in this PR since we have a couple functions that never return (e.g. raise formatted exceptions), and NoReturn lets me signal intent to pylint (e.g. https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/pull/31147/commits/f08abe1d545e14a047036fe802d8735f00986e73)

@edoakes @GeneDer This may be a behavior change, so please check this.

We set this event before `ray.killing` the controller actor. However, as far as I can tell, the only call that `awaits` this event is `graceful_shutdown`. The expected behavior for `graceful_shutdown` is that the caller waits until the controller dies, and the caller receives a `RayActorError`. There seems to be no reason to set this event since `graceful_shutdown` shouldn't return before the controller dies.

I had to remove this because there were a few tests in `test_standalone_3` that were failing (e.g. `test_controller_shutdown_gracefully`). They check that calling `graceful_shutdown` raises a `RayActorError`. These were passing because snapshotting the cluster state gave enough time for `ray.kill` to finish executing before the controller exited the control loop, which raised the `RayActorError` before `graceful_shutdown` could return. However, without snapshotting, the `graceful_shutdown` call was returning before the `RayActorError` got raised.

As further evidence that the tests failed due to a timing issue, I got the test to pass by replacing `self._put_serve_snapshot()` with `time.sleep(0.2)`.

In fact, they are not equal while preferably they should be. I think there is a problem with how interpolate_bilinear works:
- on edges
- for sub-pixel accuracy.

Right now it works like this (if we imagine white is a huge picture interpolated out of original `img`):
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8502965/82901176-de629900-9f5d-11ea-86fb-f9fa691cd0fc.png)
The value in `img` is from upper top corner of pixel in target higher resolution image. Hence on right and bottom edge, there is no much information, only extrapolation possible. This will result it right and bottom edge having some artifact, while left and top being "perfect".

Moreover, since in code there is:
```
// if the interpolation goes outside img 
if (!(left >= 0 && top >= 0 && right < img.nc() && bottom < img.nr()))
    return false;
```
in fact there won't be extrapolation happening. So the artifact won't be "same color in each row/column", but "no color setting". Worse, it affects also last values in each row/column (red cross). Hence rotating by 0 degrees produces not equal image :(

Other solution could be used where color value in `img` (blue) is in the middle of target high resolution image (white), plus clamping coordinates of 4 pixels used for interpolation (green):
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8502965/82901953-fbe43280-9f5e-11ea-84c4-b847c7850fff.png)
This would have a problem though as user would have to be aware of it and provide half pixel offset in necessary.

Maybe just clamping coordinates would be enough for now? So that interpolation uses closest existing value and always "succeeds"? It will still have asymmetrical artifact without cautious use, but at least someone forgetting to provide half pixel offset won't get wrong blurry output.
Downside with clamping might be also that it's additional condition, although can write it in a way it hints to CPU which branch will be usually taken.

Ah, I've talked with @ericl about this, the conclusion is that the 30s probably should be the minimum duration to avoid overhead to the cluster. The default GC on the local node is 10min here. And here it's to issue a global gc which is different from that one.

Besides, we think maybe the global GC should be sent from GCS since that's the place that can have better global control. For example, if all nodes are at a similar usage of memory, global GC will be triggered at the same time, and it's N*N message passing which is not necessary. Although it goes with the heartbeat, the heartbeat will be forced to be broadcasted if global_gc bit is set to be true.

I've done some work to pull out HighThroughputExecutors block provisioning code into a superclass so it can also be used by the WorkQueueExecutor. The abstraction there is roughly that that kind of executor wants to execute task by having blocks of workers allocated by a batch system, but the nature of those workers is executor specific (so in the HightThroughputExecutor case, it is our process_worker_pool.py script; in the case of workqueue, it is workqueue's own `workqueue_worker` command. That abstraction also deals with deciding (in a simple way) when more blocks should be requested.

This abstraction doesn't prevent a user from saying "don't allocate any blocks at all - I will do this myself", which makes sense if a workqueue user wants to use some other provisioning tools provided by workqueue.

It sounds like potentially that abstraction fits your flux implementation too - if so, I can push on getting this into `master` a bit harder.

I don't think `settings` is defined within the current context. Once again, the context of the template, there is a `settings.coderedcms` object holding the settings, however in the python file, that is not defined.

```python
from coderedcms.models import LayoutSettings
```
(I think that's where there are, you will have to double check). You can read the wagtail docs about how to use the wagtail settings: https://docs.wagtail.io/en/stable/reference/contrib/settings.html.  The docs will show you the difference between using them in python versus using them in the template.

---
Sort of unreleated, but also of note: The word "settings" gets used a lot and is ambiguous. So within coderedcms, we refer to Django settings (i.e. things defined in settings.py or settings/base.py, etc.) and Wagtail settings (things defined using a wagtail settings model that are then changeable by the user in the wagtail admin)

This is why `Task.register_every_n_samples_event` does this:
```
                self._every_n_acquired_event_callbacks.append(
                    callback_method_ptr)
```

The lists are declared here: https://github.com/ni/nidaqmx-python/blob/master/generated/nidaqmx/task.py#L384
```
        # These lists keep C callback objects in memory as ctypes doesn't.
        # Program will crash if callback is made after object is garbage
        # collected.
        self._done_event_callbacks = []
        self._every_n_transferred_event_callbacks = []
        self._every_n_acquired_event_callbacks = []
        self._signal_event_callbacks = []
```

Frame buffer / queue space is limited and I think the Thread spec text prescribes the device to apply "backpressure" i.e. stop sending further Answers if the frame queue reaches a certain size or frame age (? not sure).

Two ideas on this:
- First, we can rely on `Coap` module itself since it keeps the messages to retry if they fail, so if msg is dropped due to back-pressure logic, it will be retried by `Coap` module.
- Second one which I want to try out is to handle this in `NetworkDiag::Server` module itself: It sends the first answer and then waits for CoAP ack for it before sending the next answer (stop sending answer if no ack for a previous answer). Long-term IIRC, my take on the REST API was that we should always support PATCH-style behavior (even for PUT requests), i.e.:

1. If a field is skipped, it's not stored/updated, and is left as-is.
2. If a field is set to `None`, it means that its value is "cleared".
3. `1.` and `2.` apply only to top-level fields of a resource (this simplifies things for lists of objects and generally deeply-nested data).
4. In the response the full resource is returned/serialized (i.e. even fields that were not touched)

This verification has a bug: It only checks for the exact class given.

Suppose you have instantiated your circuit breaker with `CircuitBreaker(..., catch_exceptions=(Exception,))`. I think the expected behavior is to increase the counter on every error (like `TypeError` and `ValueError`, for example), right? That's not whats happening now. The way it is, if a `ValueError` is raised, the failures would not be incremented. It would only be incremented if the exact class `Exception` is raised.

To make it work as expected, you could replace this line with:
```python
if any(issubclass(exc_type, exception_class) for exception_class in self.catch_exceptions):
```

```
          |           |            |            |        |

         urn:example:animal:ferret:nose
```

Yes, there's a limited set of these: "headers" are 4-byte or 6-byte things that either just have the expected number of bytes or that plus a 2-byte instance version, and "TObject headers" vary, but they usually have `fUniqueID` and `fBits`, sometimes `pidf`, and sometimes are ingested in such a way that they also include the "headers".

Even the ability to turn on `headers=True` and `tobject_headers=True` is probably on its way out, since the AwkwardForth as-written doesn't collect them and I don't think we'll ever add that ability. Deserialization code (like AwkwardForth) needs to know about them to skip over them, but if we're not including them in the arrays anymore, we might as well also remove the ability to describe them in a Form.

That would require a deprecation cycle (setting `headers=True` or `tobject_headers=True` would raise a warning), but afterward, any member starting with an "`@`" can be filtered out here, without worrying about whether it's coming from the "headers" or the "TObject headers."

This one is more complicated than I thought.  Here's how it works:

With pop:
- inconsistent dep is removed
- none of its specs become part of the solution (and may thus be eliminated)
- package is restored, and its deps may or may not be there

Without pop:
- inconsistent dep is not factored into the solution, but it is entered in as a name spec
- solution goes ahead with any dependencies satisfied
- consistent package will get clobbered by the original inconsistent spec

We've got two different paradigms happening here:
- dict used as a "switch" block returning lambda, with the operator itself being the key
- mixin of query-modifying methods that get called via method-naming magic

Method discovery can work well in some places, but if we were to do it that way, I'd rather have it segmented into its own object and use it through composition rather than inheritance. Here, though, I'd rather see consistency with the chosen paradigm.

It's the object that is used as a fallback from the `_import()` method when the default `git show` fails. It isn't necessarily fetching anything over the network -- the data could be coming from somewhere else on the file system, if the user of this API has some special knowledge of where to fetch the data from. (Remember that this API is callable from extensions too.)

The in-tree user of this API is `west update`. That code might be running before the repository is even cloned. If that's the case, I think the right thing to do is to clone the new repository into a temporary subdirectory of .west, because we don't know where this project should go yet -- the "parent" manifest might override its `path:`. Only when all the data are available and the manifest is completely resolved can `west update` move newly-cloned repositories into their final locations.

This raises loud warning bells: if you say `[[]] * len(columns)`, then you get a list of _references to the same empty list_, but if you say `[[] for column in columns]`, then you get a list of _distinct empty lists_. That matters if those empty lists are going to be filled, and it looks like that happens below.

If the original code relied upon the lists being independent—appending to one does not append the same item to all lists—which is very likely, then it would fail if it were tested for more than one column. It's quite possible, though, that the tests don't involve more than one column and the fact that this worked shows that we're missing such a test.

```python
>>> not_what_you_want = [[]] * 5
>>> not_what_you_want
[[], [], [], [], []]
>>> not_what_you_want[0].append("wee!")
>>> not_what_you_want
[['wee!'], ['wee!'], ['wee!'], ['wee!'], ['wee!']]
```

There are two cases for this :
The user can choose whether or not to provide the location. 
 - In case they do, we take up the location they provided. 
 - In case they don't - we look for it in the local context, else, we provision it in the default location (eastus for PG and westus2 for Mysql and MariaDB). When the user does not provide the resource group, we create the resource group as well. 
Having the above validator would mean that the user **always** has to pass the resource group from which the location will be determined. However, based on the current implementation, the user can choose **not** to provide any arguments and we will create it for them. They will just issue the command - az postgres server create and we will create the resource group and in the default location and use that location for any other action that follows.

> If I understand the code correctly the `resolveAdditional` is used to track which RR types need to be added in additional section vs the ones that are already part of the answer section.
>
> Here we go through the questions, and if see there is question for TXT RR, we clear the flag for TXT in `resolveAdditional`, and then later we won't allow adding of TXT record in additional section.

You are totally correct. I used this implementation for two reasons:

- It works just fine for almost all DNS-SD client use cases. The DNS-SD client should never add unrelated names in normal use cases. 
- It is very simple to implement, without having to check which RRs have been appended to avoid duplicates. 
- It is in conformance with DNS-SD RFC because addtional RRs are a `SHOULD` requirement but not a `MUST`. It is fine that DNS-SD server do not add additoinal RRs.
- Another thought is that if the DNS-SD client do use unrelated questions, maybe it's exactly its intention **NOT** to have additional RRs. For example, if a client queries a unrated `AAAA` question, it would show that the client have some special intention for the `AAAA` resolution. This (sophisticated) client can certainly also add the related `AAAA` question itself if it wants to. 
 
> But how can we know if the questions are all for the same/related resource names?

We don't know. But they should be in most cases. 

> For example, if there is question for SRV for one name and AAA for an unrelated host name, I think we still want to include an AAAA record in additional section corresponding to host for the SRV record.
>
> Or if the question contains a PTR question and single SRV question, I think we still need to list all services in response to PTR and add a corresponding SRV record for each in the additional section except the one that may match the SRV question (and is already included in answer section)?

Yes, we should better add additional RRs for these two cases. However, this is a `SHOULD` but not `MUST` according to DNS-SD RFC. 

> Maybe before adding any RR in the additional section we add code to check in the previously prepared answer section to see if we already have a matching record (same name and type) there and if so we skip it (i.e. not duplicate it in additional section). We can use `ResourceRecord::FindRecord()` to find it in answer section. Thoughts?

This is a good implementation idea. However, I prefer the current implementation for the  reasons above. 

Thoughts? @abtink

it is useful to have both absolute and relative tolerance when comparing arrays with floating point values, because tolerance should be seen as a percentage of the original value. 
```
abs(Expected_Value - Computed_Value) < RTOL * abs(Expected_Value) + ATOL
```
eg. a change of 0.1 does not seem much when comparing 100 and 100.1. But when comparing 0.1 and 0.2 a difference of 0.1 makes a lot of difference and should ideally fail the test.

They're both dicts. The only difference is that the `message` is the `object` form, and `dumped` is the "pre-serialized" form (e.g. all enums cast to str, all fields converted to the wire form).

Running validate on `message` will error out, because the fields haven't yet been converted by the dump.

`validate` essentially ensures that you didn't forget to pass in data that needed to be there (e.g. didn't supply a required field).

I've been discussing this with offer. Currently it's designed to return 4 status codes:

200 - success, preimage in response body
400 - request was invalid (missing parameter, malformed, etc..)
404 - requested hash was not found
500 - request was fine, but the secret could not be resolved due to internal errors.

My thinking behind the retry logic is that we'd want to keep retrying the resolver endpoint until we get some sort of response indicating that the resolver received and handled our request. If there's an error, treat it as an error like was done previously and continue the flow without the resolver. The status code then would be useful in indicating why the call failed, for debugging purposes for example. `400` or `404` codes as described above would indicate that raiden is doing something wrong, for instance.

More generally speaking, I think it makes sense to intelligently handle all status codes if possible. It's a less fragile approach, especially if using other services as resolvers in the future and knowing.

The one error status code where it would make sense to me to continue retrying is `503 Service Unavailable`, although we're currently not using that. It might be wise to add it here anyway for robustness/completeness sake.

```suggestion
Data ingress starts with a function in the pandas API layer (e.g. ``read_csv``). Then the user's 
query is passed to the :doc:`Factory Dispatcher </flow/modin/core/execution/dispatching>`,
which defines a factory specific for the execution. The factory for execution contains an IO class
(e.g. ``PandasOnRayIO``) whose responsibility is to perform a parallel read/write from/to a file.
This IO class contains class methods with interfaces and names that are similar to pandas IO functions
(e.g. ``PandasOnRayIO.read_csv``). The IO class declares the Modin Dataframe and Query Compiler
classes specific for the execution engine and storage format to ensure the correct object is constructed.
It also declares IO methods that are mix-ins containing a combination of the engine-specific class for
deploying remote tasks, the class for parsing the given file format and the class handling the chunking
of the format-specific file on the head node (see dispatcher classes implementation
:doc:`details </flow/modin/core/io/index>`). The output from the IO class data ingress function is
a :doc:`Modin Dataframe </flow/modin/core/dataframe/pandas/dataframe>`.
```

Inside this PR:

```python
>>> recordarray2[2, "outer"].form
{

        "two": {
            "class": "ListOffsetArray64",

>>> awkward1.to_list(recordarray2[2, "outer"])

{"one": int64, "two": var * float64}

>>> recordarray2[2, "outer"].identities.fieldloc
[(0, 'outer')]
>>> numpy.asarray(recordarray2[2, "outer"].identities)

       [1, 0],
       [2, 0],
       [3, 0],
       [4, 0]], dtype=int32)
```

So the ListArray in one case is a ListOffsetArray in the other case—which might be benign in itself—but the Identities are clearly wrong in the PR. An array of length N should have length N Identities, but this array of length 1 has Identities of length 5. Something didn't get dereferenced.

The ListArray vs. ListOffsetArray is probably a clue. Again, having a different form in the two cases might be okay—slicing a RecordArray is different from slicing an IndexedArray, and the most optimal list type is chosen for each case. (I haven't thought deeply into _this_ case, though.) However, the fact that they're different could reveal what's different about the Identities.

At the moment, it only looks like the Identities are wrong, but they might be an indication of a deeper bug.

But I also noticed that `nomis.request_retry()` [only retry on `requests.exceptions.ConnectionError`](https://github.com/ministryofjustice/money-to-prisoners-common/blob/628260bf31eb0dae33d39b37d994e49d8ae850f4/mtp_common/nomis.py#L91).

AFAICT `requests.exceptions.ConnectionError` [would only handle](https://github.com/psf/requests/blob/master/requests/exceptions.py):
- `ProxyError`
- `SSLError`
- `ConnectTimeout`

but nothing else. Specifically it wouldn't handle `ReadTimeout` which I believe is what we're getting in our case ([example here](https://sentry.io/organizations/ministryofjustice/issues/1863733199/?environment=prod&project=2100450&referrer=alert_email)).

`ReadTimeout` inherits from `Timeout` which inherits from `RequestException`. So AFAICT that `nomis.request_retry()` would retry connection timeouts but not read timeouts 🤷

The problems I see are the following:

- The constraints that are feasible at the start of the post optimization do not necessarily stay feasible, just as the constraints that are now in `f` did not end up feasible in the first place.
- Optimizing `g**2` does neither guaranty feasibility nor even optimize for feasibility. It optimizes for getting close to the boundary of the feasible space. The latter is also what `fmin_con` does in the first place.

The suggestion in the issue "optimizing the sum of the positive constraints squared" was meant to say to optimize for ```sum([gi**2 if gi > 0 else 0 for gi in g(x)])```.

Using `es.sigma` as initial step-size seems a good alternative, or using the `es.optimize` method.

Elsewhere in this file the version number matching isn't quite so strict (e.g. `v[0-9.]*`), which would result in inconsistent behaviour if for some reason an obscure release is made such as `v53.2.6.1` (although unlikely we'd ever do such a thing and our automation probably will never support it).

I think this regexp also only allows 1 or 2 character version numbers, so e.g. v100.1.1 wouldn't be allowed (which is a number we could realistically hit).

My instinct is to be as least restrictive as reasonably possible, to allow for a more generous range of possible version numbers, so that we don't have to encode release version numbering in too many places. So I would be happy to go with `v[0-9.]*` here too, for example. However, maybe this is all rather hypothetical because we are very likely to stick with semantic versioning, and if we change, we would change the code here anyway.

Perhaps we can have a global constant that defines the regexp for the version number, and use that everywhere where we find/replace version number, to be consistent.

If you disagree, we can also go with your regexp, but let's allow numbers to three digits long, and then also use the same RE in the other lines that do a version number find/replace.

Mainly, I want to make sure that deployments that don't have explicit `runtime_env`s defined can inherit the graph-level `runtime_env`. This ensures that their code can be deserialized when their replicas start, without requiring the user to copy-paste the graph environment to each deployment in the Serve config.

Other than that, I don't feel too strongly about the particular type of inheritance behavior, as long as it's clear and well-documented. As long as users understand how the graph-level environment and deployment-level environments interact, I think either merge-based or override-based inheritance is okay.

I chose to match the existing `runtime_env` inheritance behavior, so it would be less surprising for users that are already familiar with it. For example, users might read the existing inheritance documentation and simply assume that's how inheritance works in Serve as well, so I maintained consistency.

This is a similar to TF, in that in this section of the code, the user does not have explicit control over the pre- and post-scale factors but only indirect control through `gradient_predivide_factor`. 

For the `allreduce_async_` implementation, they could set `op=Average` and both scale factors and it should work fine (i.e. the tensors will be prescaled before allreduce by `prescale_factor`, postscaled after allreduce by `postscale_factor` and divided by `size` for the average. When `op==Average`, we do not use divisor, but instead apply the additional `size` factor into `postscale_factor` in `EnqueueTensorAllreduce` here: https://github.com/horovod/horovod/blob/b0e51db4e26ea5be153cf3f3489660ebd0e310e3/horovod/common/operations.cc#L840

I'm can't think of a situation where someone would want to mix pre- and post-scale factors with `op==Average` but I do think this is doing what would be expected in that case.

The ordering is pretty important here, basically we need to:

- `reconcile_connections_pre`: Delete all connections which depend on sources or destinations that are being recreated or deleted. The underlying sources/destinations can't be deleted unless this happens first.
- `reconcile_sources/reconcile_destinations`: Update all sources and destinations, including deletion/recreation.
- `reconcile_connections_post`: (Re)create connections based on new sources and destinations.

I've added a few more comments which hopefully make this a bit more clear. If you can think of a better way to structure this, happy to take another look.

I think you are mixing up some concepts here. `Literal` is a type, so it can also be a type of a variable. It just means only specific values are allowed. In this case it would mean that `get_mode_from_gui_button()` needs to have a return type of `Literal['cg', 'cg_j', 'cg_mg', 'bf']` (or possibly a subset of those values) to guarantee that only those values will be passed to `random_walker`

I think the most intuitive explanation I can come up with is that Literals are like Enums, but without the members having a name or the enum itself having a name.

In the case of `Literal['cg', 'cg_j', 'cg_mg', 'bf'] = 'cg_j'` an Enum does seem like the more appropriate choice though. Which has the advantage that the meaning of the values can be defined and documented in one place, and every place it is used in can then also be found (by simply just looking for code that imports this enum)

The Annotated feature is something I haven't used myself yet. I think the intention for it are third party extensions for complex type checking like dependent types ( which is feature that is essential to properly type numeric code like anything that uses numpy, but that is a different topic). My intuition is that it is most likely not the solution to whatever problem you are trying to solve, but I don't fully understand the problem itself yet.

```
experimental_shell_command(
  command=f"cd {build_file_dir()} && ./script.sh",
  output_files=[f"{build_file_dir()}/../../file_that_is_elsewhere.txt"],
)
```

Effectively, that's how we'd need to implement `workdir` to solve the problem under the hood, and that would make other things a bit more complicated.

How about moving the spheroid profile calculation that creates the kernel into a separate function? That way you could test that function against the background subtraction. Something like this:
```python
def shperoid_kernel(kernel_size=(100, 100), intensity_vertex=(100,))
    kernel_size = np.array(kernel_size)
    if not np.issubdtype(kernel_size.dtype, np.number):
        raise ValueError(
            "kernel_size must be convertible to a numeric array.")
    if not kernel_size.size == 2 and len(kernel_size.shape) == 2:
        raise ValueError(
            "kernel_size must be a one dimensional array_like of size 2.")
    if np.any(kernel_size <= 0):
        raise ValueError(f"All elements of kernel_size must be greater zero.")
    kernel_size = kernel_size / 2

    intensity_vertex = np.array(intensity_vertex)
    if not np.issubdtype(intensity_vertex.dtype, np.number):
        raise ValueError(
            "Intensity_vertex must be convertible to a numeric array.")
    if not intensity_vertex.size == 1 and len(intensity_vertex.shape) == 1:
        raise ValueError(
            "Intensity_vertex must be a scalar.")
    if np.any(intensity_vertex <= 0):
        raise ValueError(f"Intensity_vertex must be greater zero.")
    intensity_vertex = intensity_vertex / 2

    kernel_size_y, kernel_size_x = np.round(kernel_size).astype(int)

    tmp_x = np.arange(-kernel_size_x, kernel_size_x + 1)
    tmp_y = np.arange(-kernel_size_y, kernel_size_y + 1)
    x, y = np.meshgrid(tmp_x, tmp_y)

    kernel_size_y, kernel_size_x = kernel_size
    tmp = (x / kernel_size_x) ** 2 + (y / kernel_size_y) ** 2
    cap_height = intensity_vertex - intensity_vertex * \
        np.sqrt(np.clip(1 - tmp, 0, None))

    kernel = np.array(tmp <= 1, dtype=float)
    kernel[kernel == 0] = np.Inf
    return kernel


def test_spheroid_gradient():
    # spheroid image
    img = spheroid_kernel(kernel_size=(100, 100), intensity_vertex=(100,))

    result = rolling_ball(img, radius=5)
    assert np.allclose(result, np.zeros_like(img))

def test_small_spheroid_gradient():
    # spheroid image
    img = spheroid_kernel(kernel_size=(5, 5), intensity_vertex=(5,)) # this feature may have to be embedded into a larger image

    result = rolling_ball(img, radius=100)
    assert not np.allclose(result, np.zeros_like(img))
```
This should definitely only be in addition to the other tests, but it is a nice way to confirm that the background subtraction works as intended

This is a change applied by the black formatter. black makes this change only in versions [23.1.0](https://black.readthedocs.io/en/stable/change_log.html#id8) and higher. 23.1.0 is the version that adheres to black's [2023 stable style](https://github.com/psf/black/issues/3407). I suspect but did not validate that [this change to empty line handling](https://github.com/psf/black/pull/3302) which is included in the 2023 stable style causes this change to be applied. botocore specifies the black version used for CI in [the precommit config file](https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/a91a7bf/.pre-commit-config.yaml#L37). 

I decided to leave the change in for two reasons:
1. More and more contributors will update their local black version to >=23.1.0 and this change will continue sneaking into diffs (and then require unnecessary attention).
2. Versions of black <23.1.0 do not revert this change, so there is downside to keeping it.

Yeah, I'm saying that this `output_queue.join()` is nonsensical, since it should never block, and if it ever did (e.g. due to a bug), it would block forever.

All that `Queue.join()` does is block until all work is done, i.e. for every item put in the queue, an `output_queue.task_done()` call has been made; this is why this is typically called in the producer thread (the thread calling `output_queue.put()`), since it's waiting for the consumer(s) to be done doing work. But here we're calling it in the consumer thread (the thread calling `output_queue.get()` and `output_queue.task_done()`), and if `output_queue.join()` ever blocks, it would block forever, since no other threads can call `output_queue.task_done()` to unblock it.

I'm saying that I think we should remove the `output_queue.join()` call here. Does that make sense?

unsafe APIs appear to be treated as a language feature, not like the rest of the standard library where the build tag approach would suffice.

Also, just to clarify: it's not a breaking change to bump the `go` directive in the go.mod. It's not a minimum required version enforcement like other languages. It's just a "code was written assuming these semantics" directive. If a module says `go 1.20` a 1.19 compiler can still try to compile and use it. So if you bump the directive to 1.20, but add a file tagged `//go:build !go1.20` to make the code compatible with 1.19, it'll still work as expected.
All that said, it's probably not worth the effort to do all this just to bump the go directive right now. We're 3 months to the next release, at which point Go 1.20 will be the minimum supported version anyway.

It was more of a convention/syntax highlighting fix.

In general, all body of text supplied to directive should go on a new indented line. If the directive takes arguments then that should go on the same line as the directive (for required args) or in the directive's options. RST comments should follow the same convention
```rst
..
    comment goes here (not inline with ``..``)
```

Lastly, rst highlighters have problems with text that was started on the same line as the directive then continued on the next line using the same indent
```rst
.. warning:: This is not a required arg. It is the body which
             is continued with an awkward indent.
```

We need a compromise here:
- `ABC` forces you into inheritance, which is defeating protocol purpose (especially https://github.com/commitizen-tools/commitizen/pull/733#discussion_r1182835871) and it would force `Pep440` and `SemVer` into multiple inheritance
- `Protocol` is the opposite, it forces into nothing because by default it is ignored at runtime

However, there is one possibility which is interesting: we can make the protocol [runtime checkable](https://docs.python.org/3/library/typing.html#typing.runtime_checkable) and raise a warning or fail when calling `get_version_scheme`, something like:

```python
scheme = ep.load()
try:
    isinstance(scheme, VersionProtocol)
except TypeError:
    warnings.warn(f"{name} is not implementing the Version protocol")
return scheme
```

This way we would have best of both world:
- clean type hinting
- freedom of implementation
- runtime error or warning for devs not using MyPy

According to my test, the `read_all` method returns `b''` if there is no data to be read out.

So the `if new_data is not None:` should be changed to `if new_data:`, otherwise it will never do `time.sleep`, which will cause high CPU usage.

```py
    def _read(self, timeout: Optional[float]) -> Optional[str]:
        _timeout = serial.Timeout(timeout)

        with error_check("Could not read from serial device"):
            while not _timeout.expired():
                new_data = self.serialPortOrig.read_all()
                if new_data:
                    self._buffer.extend(new_data)
                else:
                    time.sleep(0.001)
                    continue

                for terminator in (self._ERROR, self._OK):
                    if terminator in self._buffer:
                        i = self._buffer.index(terminator) + 1
                        string = self._buffer[:i].decode()
                        del self._buffer[:i]
                        return string

            return None
```

```
# on all OSes
assert string.Template("$$").safe_substitute() == "$"

# on Unix
assert os.path.expandvars("$$") == "$$"

# on Windows
assert os.path.expandvars("$$") == "$"
```

Because of this I ended up implementing a new function that properly handles `$VAR`, `${VAR}`, and `%VAR%` and leaves all other patterns as-is.

I'm just trying to make sure that I'm understanding the concern correctly. Is the question about whether we should just remove this condition and always re-calculate a new timer value?

Right now, the logic I'm trying to implement is that if the new `aIntervalMax` is smaller than our running timer, we will adjust it to fire at the original start time + `aIntervalMax` (emulating our last random selection picking the `aIntervalMax` value). If the running timer is less than `aIntervalMax` we just let it run to its original duration as the original duration will still meet the bounds criteria.

My goal is demonstrated with few examples below (assume a start time of 0 for all):

| `mInterval` | `aIntervalMax` | current time |  Action | 
|--|--|--|--|
| 10000 | 15000 | 100 | timer fires at 10000 |
| 15000 | 15000 | 100 | timer fires at 15000 | 
| 20000 | 15000 | 100 | timer fires at 15000 |
| 20000 | 5000 | 10000 | timer fires at 10000 |

Looking at the code for the scheduler that `TimerMilli` uses leads me to believe that a timer with a historical fire time would be placed first in the queue of the scheduler and would fire immediately next time the scheduler checks for items which need to run.

If I've implemented this correctly, and my logic seems reasonable, the result of the contrived example using `SetIntervalMax` with a smaller `aIntervalMax` (which is always greater than `mInterval`) over time is that it will fire the timer at the original time and then on the next cycle it will continue with the trickle mechanism of attempting to increase the time, but will use a new maximum value.

In the case that `aIntervalMax` is less than `mInterval` it would trigger an immediate fire of the timer and the continue on with the trickle algorithm with the `mIntervalMax` having the value `aIntervalMax`

> `literal_eval` in line 619 in cli.py changes the hex to integer. Hence we will have to re-hex it. Hence this change will not work with the current cli.py file.

The problem is that re-hex an integer to a string will miss the leading zeros which causes the error of `non-hexadecimal number found` in bytes.fromhex().
For example:
```python3
original_hash = '0x0b95d228'
hash_integer = int(original_hash, 16)
rehexed_hash = hex(hash_integer) # 0xb95d228, missing the leading zeros compared to original_hash
```

FYI: Python 3 enums remember the order in which values are defined and allow iterating over the values in definition order at run time.
https://docs.python.org/3/library/enum.html#enum.EnumType.__iter__

However, Python 2 enums didn't remember definition order by default. You had to set the `_order_` attribute. So these enums were not always ordered.

Changing the enum value order breaks compatibility, but very few DAQmx applications are likely to depend on this, and upgrading from Python 2 to Python 3 already changed this behavior.

As a side note. Passing secrets via environment variables is something that's really frowned upon (since the secrets still leak via `/proc/$PID/environ` to all child processes), which is why systemd added the credentials feature, which I use in #100, but the lower version bound is still a bit too low for that. ```python
```python
def _add_indicator(
    indicator: Union[bool, str],
    how: str,
    index_size: int,
    nlevels: int,
    columns: pd.Index,
) -> Tuple[Union[str, Tuple[str, ...]], pd.Categorical]:
    """Add an indicator column to the DataFrame.

    Args:
        indicator: Indicator column name or True for default name "_merge".
        how: Type of join operation ("inner", "left", "right").
        index_size: Size of the index on which the indicator is based.
        nlevels: Number of levels in the DataFrame's column MultiIndex.
        columns: Union of the columns from both DataFrames.

    Returns:
        A tuple containing the indicator column name and a Categorical array
        representing the indicator values for each row.

    Raises:
        ValueError: If the indicator name is already present in the columns.

    """
    mapping = {"left": "left_only", "right": "right_only", "inner": "both"}
    categories = ["left_only", "right_only", "both"]

    if isinstance(indicator, bool):
        indicator = "_merge"

    if indicator in columns:
        raise ValueError(
            "Cannot use the name of an existing column for the indicator column."
        )

    if nlevels > 1:
        indicator = (indicator,) + ("",) * (nlevels - 1)

    if not index_size:
        arr = pd.Categorical([], categories=categories)
    else:
        arr = pd.Categorical(
            [mapping[how]],
            categories=categories,
        )
        if index_size > 1:
            arr = arr.repeat(index_size)

    return indicator, arr
```

> 2\. The upper limit for a resource is the resource allocated when executing the Nextflow script. In this example, 4 CPUs and 16GBs of memory.

Ok, that answers the gist of my question. We can only sub-allocate resources here. Nextflow's Slurm executor does not get to create new Slurm jobs as first-class citizens. It _is_ a controlled vocabulary, but the chosen values get passed on directly to Slurm.

> So, I am not sure whether problems may arise from the fact that I am requesting the `medium` partition inside the `batch_script.sh` but then trying to execute the binarization processor inside the `gpu` partition.

Based on your description, I doubt it will work. (Slurm knows job dependencies and can thus chain jobs on different partitions, but you cannot "break out" of a partition AFAIK.)

> Overall, the hard part is to estimate the required resources for each step based on the provided workspace. 

I know. Very hard. Set it too low, and it will crash and you'll have to restart. Set it too high and the job will take much longer to be scheduled, and likely waste time (which cannot be otherwise filled by the scheduler). And esp. memory depends on the data, even various from page to page.

That's why I liked your NF repeat escalator so much. But it does not help us here – we'd have to determine the overall resources in the script beforehand anyway.

> To provide more flexible configurations, probably it would be required to produce the batch scripts and the nextflow config files dynamically.

Yes, that I was hoping the NF Slurm executor would do.

> I think this post is generally more relevant for Operandi, but it still provides (hopefully) useful insights about the HPC environment, Nextflow, and OCR-D in general.

We came here by discussing NF's own means of error handling. It's still the right place IMO, as it's about the overall architecture.

Let's keep this in the agent for now. We should raise this with others on the Serve team, and potentially move the Serve REST API as a whole in a separate PR.

For context, the Serve REST API used to be on the head node under a `serve_head.py` file. However, it was moved to the dashboard agent to provide better fault tolerance in case the head node (and the dashboard) crashes. Any higher-level service executor (e.g. KubeRay) needs access to the REST API to check Serve's health. If the API is in the head node, then anytime the head node goes down, Serve's health check would fail, and the executor would restart Serve instead of waiting for it to recover. By putting the REST API in the dashboard agent, the executor can check Serve's health at other cluster nodes while the head node is recovering.

Sorry if the commit message was a bit unclear. Here gemm computes:

out = x @ y + beta * out

And by default [`beta = 1.0`](https://github.com/explosion/cython-blis/blob/e42ef40b7af784a18f08fe6d3cc35680988613d2/blis/py.pyx#L66). So, without this change, this computes

out = x @ y + 1.0 * out

So, `out` is added to the matrix multiplication of `x` and `y`. This is not the case when BLIS is not used.

Thanks for taking the time to give this some thought. I had meant these changes as a fairly low-key way of documenting how to put license/copyright data in a file header if people wanted to, rather than a below-the-radar attempt to significantly change the norms/practices for Kolibri. The scope I had in mind for this was only for future contributions to Kolibri, and not retrospectively adding copyright/license headers to existing files.

I think it would be great if someone _did_ retrospectively add headers to existing files, but it’s a *lot* of work and imo only worthwhile when there’s a strong use case for sorting it out — perhaps if a prospective big government user of Kolibri had software licensing reporting requirements to meet before they could use it. That kind of use case is one of the motivations for REUSE existing. The work comes from doing the git archaeology to work out the copyright holders of each file. In another project I work on [we’ve been slowly chipping away at this](https://gitlab.gnome.org/GNOME/glib/-/issues/1415) over time, with CI in place to never allow the REUSE compliance level (as reported by `reuse lint`) to decrease. I am not proposing that here now though.

REUSE allows for using `.reuse/dep5`, but it’s mildly [discouraged](https://reuse.software/faq/#bulk-license) compared to putting the information in the file headers where possible. My take on it is that the use of a `dep5` file keeps the license/copyright separate from the code, so it doesn’t solve the problem of that metadata going missing if someone copies code out of Kolibri into another project. It also makes it less likely that people will add themselves to the copyright for a file when they make a copyrightable contribution to it, because that metadata will be far away from what they’re editing. That reduces the chance of the copyright listings staying up to date.

Using a `dep5` file is an improvement on freeform documentation of the license in `LICENSE`, though, as it is machine readable. It can also be iterated on in future to refine the copyright listings.

I can completely appreciate you don’t want to change existing working practices, or have another thing to remember to remind people about when reviewing PRs. How about changing the wording in this PR from “it’s encouraged to list copyright and license data explicitly” to “it’s supported to list copyright and license data explicitly”? For the reasons above, I think steering people towards `dep5` is a red herring.

I think it is for supporting both NCP mode and RCP mode(posix host + RCP firmware). In RCP mode, `SubMac.cpp` will be included in **both** posix host and RCP firmware. In host side, it will get the radio capability from `SubMac::GetCaps` which combines the real radio capability and the SW capability as the `SubMac` can add SW capability which is missed in radio. If `posix host` finds RCP supports `OT_RADIO_CAPS_RECEIVE_TIMING`, it should not start the receive timer and leave it to RCP. On RCP side, this could be done in `SubMac` layer or radio layer, depends on whether radio has the capability or not in `Get<Radio>().GetCaps()`. If radio supports it, it will leave it to radio and should not start receive timer, if not, it will handle it in `SubMac` layer.
